# COLOR WHEEL WEDNESDAY Ep. 2 — The Web Wheel

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube Shorts)
**Date:** 2026-03-18
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/shorts/B1tfImuPTcA

## Description

The Web Wheel — a time capsule tool for harmonizing the 216 "web-safe" colors from the early days of web design. Published around 2000 by the Color Wheel Company.

## Transcript

It's color wheel Wednesday.

This week we're talking about the web wheel. The web wheel, like most color wheels, is both misguided and utterly charming. Published around the year 2000 by the Color Wheel Company. It's kind of an unintentional time capsule. There's only a span of about 10 years in all of human history when a person working on web graphics might want a physically printed harmony guide.

Back in my day, we didn't have all these fancy color picker apps. 1996, Linda Weinman named the palette of colors that the Netscape, Internet Explorer, and Mosaic browsers could all correctly render. She named that palette the "browser safe palette." And by about the year 2000, most people were calling it the "web safe palette" or the "web safe colors."

This small cube icon indicates that the current color that we have chosen in our color picker is not web safe. The 216 colors were all made with a limited set of hex values: 00, 33, 66, 99, CC, and FF in different combinations. The idea was you wanted to design with those browser limitations in mind.

So this wheel not only listed the hex codes for you but also laid out the colors according to the RGB model rather than the red-yellow-blue model, and it showed complementary pairs like magenta-green as opposed to red-green.

Are web safe colors still relevant? Well, you know, most monitors today have 24-bit color. They can render millions of colors, and so designers don't really have to think in terms of these limitations anymore. But interestingly, you can still find this fossil of the early days of web graphics in some form in Adobe Photoshop. In your color picker, you can turn on "make ramp web" and it reduces you to 15 shades of red, orange, yellow, etc. Rotating the hue shows you this nifty pattern as the dominance of red, green, and blue shift by hue around the wheel.

I was actually inspired by this odd pattern of slopes to build this tool. It's supposed to help people visualize the difference between saturation and chroma. But when I showed it to my wife, she said, "I didn't understand a word you just said." So, I'm still working on it.

## Key Facts

- **216 web-safe colors** — built from 6 hex values per channel: `00`, `33`, `66`, `99`, `CC`, `FF` (6×6×6 = 216)
- **Linda Weinman** coined "browser safe palette" in 1996
- **The Web Wheel** — physical printed color harmony guide by the Color Wheel Company (~2000), used RGB model, showed complementary pairs (magenta-green, not red-green)
- **Photoshop fossil** — "make ramp web" option still exists in the color picker
- **~10-year window** (~1996–2006) when web-safe colors mattered
